title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
This review assesses the submission, a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds. The proposed method consists of a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The submission is well-written, and the proposed approach achieves state-of-the-art results on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets.

The paper presents a thorough analysis of the proposed method, addressing the challenging problem of 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds. The authors provide a clear motivation for their approach, thoroughly evaluating it on two benchmark datasets. The proposed V2B tracker effectively leverages the strengths of both Siamese networks and voxel-to-BEV transformation to improve tracking performance, especially in sparse scenes.

One of the key strengths of the paper is its comprehensive ablation study, which validates the effectiveness of the proposed method. However, as noted by Alternative Review 2, a more thorough comparison with recent works, especially those using anchor-free detection methods, would further strengthen the paper. Additionally, the authors could provide more insights into the limitations of their approach, such as the reliance on high-quality template and search area point clouds.

The paper's clarity and organization are notable, making it easy to follow. However, to further improve, the authors could provide more detailed analysis of failure cases and discuss the limitations of their method, as suggested by Alternative Review 4. Moreover, exploring the potential negative societal impact of their work, such as the use of tracking technology in surveillance, would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the proposed method's implications.

The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets. However, as mentioned in the main review, some claims, such as the superiority of the proposed method in handling sparse point clouds, require further justification. A more detailed analysis of the proposed method's performance in various scenarios, including dynamic environments, would provide a more comprehensive understanding of its strengths and limitations.

In conclusion, the paper presents a well-structured and thorough analysis of the proposed Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker. While it achieves state-of-the-art results and demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method, there are areas for improvement, such as providing more detailed analysis of failure cases and exploring the potential negative societal impact of the proposed method. Overall, the paper is well-written, and the proposed method shows promise in addressing the challenging problem of 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds.

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. The proposed model consists of two main modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. While the paper outperforms preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

The proposed method clearly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets, which is a significant achievement. As noted in Alternative Review 1, the authors provide a clear motivation for their approach and thoroughly evaluate it on two benchmark datasets. The use of a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network effectively leverages the strengths of both Siamese networks and voxel-to-BEV transformation to improve tracking performance, especially in sparse scenes.

However, I have concerns about the writing quality. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences, which detract from the overall clarity of the paper. As Alternative Review 2 suggests, a more thorough comparison with recent works, especially those using anchor-free detection methods, would be beneficial. Additionally, the impact of hyperparameters on the tracking performance could be analyzed in more detail.

Regarding the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks, I agree with the main review that it is a complicated module with heavy engineering of features without a detailed ablation study. As Alternative Review 3 notes, the paper could benefit from a more comprehensive ablation study to validate the effectiveness of each component. Specifically, ablation of global/local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity maps may be useful.

Furthermore, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection, such as PointPillar and SECOND. The authors must cite these papers and provide a clear explanation of how their work differs from these contributions.

In terms of limitations and societal impact, I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitations or societal impact of this work. As Alternative Review 4 notes, the authors could provide more detailed analysis of the failure cases and discuss the limitations of their method. Additionally, the authors should consider providing more insights into the potential risks and limitations of their method, such as its performance in dynamic environments or its computational efficiency.

Despite these concerns, I believe that the paper has the potential to make a significant impact in the field. The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art results, and the authors provide a clear motivation for their approach. However, I maintain my rating of 6, Marginally above the acceptance threshold, as the paper requires significant revisions to address the concerns mentioned above.

## Rating
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker for 3D single object tracking on sparse point clouds, addressing a crucial aspect of autonomous driving. The proposed method consists of a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the existing methods and clearly explain the motivation and contribution of their work.

The main strength of this paper lies in its ability to effectively tackle the challenging problem of 3D single object tracking on sparse point clouds. The proposed V2B tracker achieves state-of-the-art results on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets, outperforming existing methods. The authors provide a clear and well-structured presentation of their work, making it easy to follow and understand.

One of the key contributions of this paper is the Siamese shape-aware feature learning network, which captures 3D shape information of the object to learn discriminative features. The voxel-to-BEV target localization network accurately regresses the target's 2D center and z-axis center from a dense BEV feature map. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, with significant improvements over existing state-of-the-art approaches.

However, there are some areas that require further discussion. The authors mention that the performance on the cyclist category is worse than other categories, and they attribute this to the lack of training data. It would be interesting to explore this further and investigate whether there are any potential biases in the dataset. Additionally, the authors could provide more insights into the limitations of their method, such as its performance in dynamic environments or its computational efficiency.

The authors should also consider providing more detailed analysis of failure cases and discussing potential avenues for future work. For instance, they could explore the use of additional data sources, such as RGB images, to improve the tracking performance. Furthermore, they could discuss the potential applications and implications of their work in real-world scenarios, such as autonomous driving and robotics.

In terms of limitations, the authors have adequately addressed the challenges of tracking objects in sparse point clouds. However, they could further discuss the potential risks and limitations of their method, such as its reliance on high-quality point cloud data and its potential vulnerability to adversarial attacks.

Overall, this paper presents a well-structured and effective approach to 3D single object tracking on sparse point clouds. The proposed V2B tracker demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets, and the authors provide a thorough analysis of their method. With some additional discussion on the limitations and potential avenues for future work, this paper has the potential to make a significant impact in the field.

Rating: 6 

The paper proposes a novel approach with significant potential, but may require some additional work to address the limitations and potential risks. The rating of 6 reflects the paper's potential, but also acknowledges that there are areas for improvement. 

The main contributions of this work are:
- A novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D single object tracking on sparse point clouds
- State-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets

The main limitations of this work are:
- Limited analysis of failure cases
- Potential biases in the dataset
- Limited discussion of potential risks and limitations

The paper is well-written and provides a clear presentation of the proposed method and its evaluation. However, there are areas for improvement, and the authors should consider addressing these limitations in future work. 

The final rating is 6, which reflects the paper's potential and the need for additional work to address the limitations and potential risks. 

The decision to accept this paper is based on its potential impact, the significance of the problem it addresses, and the effectiveness of the proposed approach. However, the authors should be encouraged to address the limitations and potential risks in future work. 

The rating is 6, marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Summary:
This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.

Main Review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?

I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.

reviews.text:
This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds, adopting a tracking by detection framework implemented with a Siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. The authors embed the template feature into the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss, and use a dense detection approach to directly localize the target from dense BEV voxel locations.

The paper presents a well-structured and well-written work that addresses the challenging problem of 3D single object tracking on sparse point clouds. However, there are areas that need improvement. The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal-based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points.

A clear comparison with previous methods is necessary. The authors could provide more insights into how their method differs from P2B and SC3D, and what specific changes they made to improve performance. For instance, a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D would help justify these modifications and clarify the technical novelty. Additionally, qualitative results, such as those suggested by Alternative Review 1, could be added to show examples of improvements, especially in sparse scenes.

The method section is hard to follow, with tons of technical details thrown at the readers without providing enough context and motivation for why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors re-write the method section in the revised submission, providing more detailed explanations and justifications for their design choices.

The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, but there can be more results to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, an ablation study with SC3D, as mentioned earlier, and qualitative comparisons with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist category, where the proposed method performs much worse compared to SC3D. Furthermore, Alternative Review 2 points out the need for a more thorough comparison with recent works, especially those using anchor-free detection methods.

The authors should also provide more insights into the limitations of their approach and potential avenues for future work. As Alternative Review 1 suggests, discussing potential negative societal impacts, such as the use of their technology for surveillance or tracking individuals without consent, and providing concrete suggestions for mitigating these risks would be beneficial.

Regarding the computational efficiency, Alternative Review 2 mentions that the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, but Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU. The authors should clarify the reason for this huge gap and provide more details on the computational resources required for their method.

In conclusion, while the paper presents a novel approach to 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds, there are areas that need improvement. With a clearer comparison with previous methods, a better presentation of the method section, more results in the experiments, and a discussion of limitations and potential negative societal impacts, this paper can be further strengthened.

reviews.text:
This paper proposes a Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker for 3D single object tracking on sparse point clouds. The proposed method consists of a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The former captures 3D shape information of the object to learn discriminative features, while the latter regresses the target's 2D center and z-axis center from a dense BEV feature map.

The paper presents a well-structured and well-written work that addresses the challenging problem of 3D single object tracking on sparse point clouds. The authors provide a clear motivation for their approach and thoroughly evaluate it on two benchmark datasets, KITTI and nuScenes. The proposed V2B tracker effectively leverages the strengths of both Siamese networks and voxel-to-BEV transformation to improve tracking performance, especially in sparse scenes.

One of the key strengths of this work is its thorough evaluation on two benchmark datasets. The authors provide a detailed analysis of the experimental results, which demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. However, as noted by Alternative Review 2, the paper could benefit from a more thorough comparison with recent works, especially those using anchor-free detection methods.

The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art results on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets. However, as mentioned in the main review, the novelty of the proposed method is limited, as it combines well-known components, such as Siamese networks and voxel-to-BEV transformation. Nevertheless, the authors' implementation and evaluation of the method are thorough, and the results are impressive.

Ablation studies are also crucial in evaluating the effectiveness of the proposed method. As noted by Alternative Review 1, the authors provide an ablation study that validates the effectiveness of the proposed method. However, it would be beneficial to provide more insights into the limitations of the proposed method, such as its performance in dynamic environments or its computational efficiency.

The authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. They discuss the challenges of tracking objects in sparse point clouds and provide a thorough analysis of the existing methods. However, as noted by Alternative Review 4, the authors could further discuss the potential applications and implications of their work in real-world scenarios, such as autonomous driving and robotics.

In conclusion, the proposed V2B tracker is a well-designed and effective method for 3D single object tracking on sparse point clouds. While the novelty of the proposed method is limited, the authors' implementation and evaluation of the method are thorough, and the results are impressive. With some minor revisions to address the limitations and potential negative societal impact of the work, this paper would be suitable for publication.

Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)

This rating reflects the paper's strengths in proposing a well-designed and effective method for 3D single object tracking on sparse point clouds, as well as its thorough evaluation on benchmark datasets. However, the paper's novelty is limited, and some minor revisions are needed to address the limitations and potential negative societal impact of the work.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method, which incorporates a shape completion technique to enhance feature learning in sparse point cloud scenarios. The proposed method, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker, consists of a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The former captures 3D shape information of the object to learn discriminative features, while the latter regresses the target's 2D center and z-axis center from a dense bird's eye view (BEV) feature map.

The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive and significantly improve performance, as shown in the Table 4 ablation experiments. This is in line with the Alternative Review 1's comment on the effectiveness of the V2B tracker in leveraging the strengths of both Siamese networks and voxel-to-BEV transformation. The qualitative analysis on results for sparse point clouds intuitively demonstrates the usefulness of the proposed method, which aligns with Alternative Review 2's suggestion for a thorough comparison with recent works.

However, there are several areas that require improvement. The main contribution of the paper, the shape completion technique, is not thoroughly compared with prior work, such as [13]. The authors claim that their method performs shape completion in the search area, whereas [13] performs shape completion for the template. Nevertheless, it is unclear if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. To address this, the authors could add shape completion for the template in their proposed method and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]'s codebase. This is a crucial comparison to make, as Alternative Review 2 suggests, to validate the effectiveness of the proposed shape completion technique.

Moreover, the voxel-to-BEV target localization network essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear how this feature max pool on the z-axis helps the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center. In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information, so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the max-pooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper's claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center becomes more effective and accurate. The authors should provide a detailed analysis of this voxel-to-BEV ablation, including an experiment showing that after adding or not adding voxel-to-BEV, the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) changes significantly.

Another concern is the network design. The paper extensively uses PointNet++, which is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). As Alternative Review 2 suggests, more advanced point cloud processing methods could be used to improve the performance. The authors should justify their choice of network architecture and consider using more recent methods.

Furthermore, the evaluation protocol is limited, with testing only performed on two sequences of KITTI. As Alternative Review 3 and Alternative Review 4 suggest, this is not sufficient for a robust evaluation. The authors should consider adding another dataset, such as nuScenes or Waymo, for evaluation and comparison with other methods. This would increase the confidence in the results and provide a more comprehensive evaluation of the proposed method.

In terms of limitations and societal impact, the authors have adequately addressed the limitations of their work. However, they could further discuss the potential negative societal impact of their work, such as the use of 3D tracking in surveillance applications, and provide more concrete suggestions for mitigating these risks.

Overall, while the paper presents a novel approach to 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds, there are several areas that require improvement. With revisions addressing these concerns, the paper could make a significant contribution to the field.

reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

reviews.text:
The paper proposes a Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker for 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds. The V2B tracker consists of a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The former captures 3D shape information of the object, while the latter regresses the target's 2D center and z-axis center from a dense BEV feature map.

The paper is well-written, and the idea is clear. However, I have several concerns that need to be addressed. Firstly, the application focus of the proposed technique is not entirely clear. The authors claim that their method is for 3D object tracking, but it seems more like a 3D detection method. The authors need to clarify how their method differs from existing 3D object tracking methods.

One of the major concerns is that the paper does not provide a thorough comparison with state-of-the-art methods. The authors only compare their method with a few existing methods, but there are many other methods that they do not consider. For example, they do not compare their method with the methods proposed in [1] and [2], which are highly relevant to their work.

The authors also need to provide more details about their experimental setup. For instance, they mention that they use the KITTI dataset for evaluation, but they do not provide enough information about how they preprocess the data. Additionally, they do not provide a thorough analysis of their method's performance on different types of objects.

Furthermore, the paper lacks a detailed discussion of the limitations of the proposed method. The authors should discuss the potential limitations of their method, such as its performance in dynamic environments or its computational efficiency.

In terms of related work, the authors should also consider including a more comprehensive review of existing 3D object tracking methods. This would help to better position their work and demonstrate its novelty.

The authors should also provide more insights into the potential applications of their method. For example, they could discuss how their method could be used in autonomous vehicles or robotics.

Overall, the paper has some good ideas, but it needs significant improvements in terms of clarity, thoroughness, and evaluation. I would recommend that the authors revise their paper to address these concerns.

Section 3 presents the proposed V2B tracker. Section 4 evaluates the proposed method on several benchmark datasets. Section 5 concludes the paper and discusses future work.

The proposed V2B tracker has several advantages over existing methods. Firstly, it uses a Siamese network to learn a similarity metric between the object and the background. This allows the tracker to effectively distinguish between the object and the background. Secondly, the tracker uses a voxel-to-BEV transformation to project the 3D point cloud into a 2D BEV feature map. This allows the tracker to effectively capture the 3D structure of the object.

However, the proposed method also has some limitations. Firstly, it assumes that the object is rigid and does not deform over time. Secondly, it assumes that the object is not occluded by other objects. These assumptions may not always hold in practice.

To address these limitations, future work could focus on developing more robust tracking algorithms that can handle non-rigid objects and occlusions. Additionally, future work could focus on developing more efficient tracking algorithms that can run in real-time.

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

The paper analyzes the generalization properties of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithms for supervised learning problems. The authors focus on two settings: (1) the new task at test time is one of the training tasks, and (2) the task at test time is new and unseen. The paper proposes a novel definition of stability for meta-learning algorithms, which allows the authors to capture the role of both the number of tasks and the number of samples per task on the generalization error of MAML.

The authors provide a thorough analysis of the generalization properties of MAML algorithms. They propose a new stability definition, called (γ, K)-uniformly stable, which is compatible with the MAML setting. This definition allows the authors to derive a generalization error bound that decays at a rate of O(1/mn) for strongly convex objective functions. The authors also provide a bound on the generalization error of MAML when the task at test time is new and unseen, which depends on the total variation distance between the underlying distributions of the new task and the tasks observed during training.

One of the key contributions of this paper is the introduction of a new notion of stability that captures the dependence on the number of tasks and samples. This notion is a significant improvement over existing work, as it provides a more nuanced understanding of the generalization properties of MAML algorithms. The authors also provide a detailed analysis of the related work and compare their results with existing bounds.

However, as noted by Alternative Review 1, the paper assumes strong convexity of the loss functions, which may limit the applicability of the results. The authors acknowledge this limitation and discuss potential avenues for future work, including the development of MAML algorithms that can handle non-convex loss functions.

The paper is well-written and easy to follow, with clear explanations of the technical concepts and notation. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the generalization properties of MAML algorithms and discuss the implications of their results. The paper also provides a detailed comparison with existing work and highlights the significance of the proposed approach.

In terms of limitations, the paper assumes that the number of tasks is large, which may not always be the case in practice. Additionally, the paper does not provide a detailed analysis of the computational complexity of the proposed approach. These limitations are acknowledged by the authors and provide a starting point for future work.

The main weaknesses of the analysis are the assumptions of strong convexity and the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. However, the authors provide a thorough discussion of these limitations and suggest potential avenues for future work.

Overall, the paper provides a significant contribution to the understanding of MAML algorithms and their generalization properties. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the related work and compare their results with existing bounds. The paper is well-written, and the results are clearly presented.

The rating of 6, as in the main review, reflects the significance of the contributions made by this paper. While there are some limitations to the analysis, the paper provides a thorough understanding of the generalization properties of MAML algorithms and suggests avenues for future work.

Rating: 6

The paper provides a significant contribution to the understanding of MAML algorithms and their generalization properties. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the related work and compare their results with existing bounds. The paper is well-written, and the results are clearly presented. 

The main contributions of this paper are:

*   A new notion of stability for MAML algorithms that captures the dependence on the number of tasks and samples.
*   A thorough analysis of the generalization properties of MAML algorithms.
*   A detailed comparison with existing work and a discussion of the implications of the results.

The paper also highlights some potential avenues for future work, including:

*   Extending the analysis to non-convex loss functions.
*   Developing MAML algorithms that can handle large numbers of tasks.

Overall, the paper provides a significant contribution to the understanding of MAML algorithms and their generalization properties. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the related work and compare their results with existing bounds. The paper is well-written, and the results are clearly presented.

reviews.summary: This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.

Summary:
This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.

Main Review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.

No limitation or potential negative societal impact is shown.
