title: Reinforcement learning for instance segmentation with high-level priors

reviews.summary: This paper presents a strategy for object instance segmentation from images using training data that has no manual annotations. Instead of manual annotations, as is required for many state-of-the-art methods, this paper proposes to use high-level priors represented as rules that can be evaluated in the produced segmentation. These rules define what an acceptable segmentation is, and are used to create a reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent. The framework formulates an environment, an agent, an action space and a reward system in a stateless system to guide segmentation. All the components of the system are well designed and well thought, and this approach may have wide applications in bioimage segmentation problems.
reviews.main_review:
* The stateless actor-critic model has connections with the dynamics of Generative Adversarial Networks, as the authors note. The fact that there is no state also begs the question of what are the connections with the REINFORCE rule for training models when the loss function is non-differentiable, and how that approach could simplify or enhance the proposed formulation.
* The experimental design is interesting and overall complete. However, the performance metrics used for evaluation are not in line with research in the instance segmentation field, where precision and recall are used in several different ways. For example, computing precision or F1 score at specific intersection over union thresholds would be more informative to understand how many objects / regions are correctly segmented and to what level of overlap with ground truth.
* There are a few datasets studied in the instance segmentation literature that could have been used as benchmarks in this evaluation. At least one dataset used for nucleus segmentation of cells, for instance, would add a lot of value to the paper for two reasons: first, it connects with previous literature in the topic, second, it allows to assess how far a model trained without ground truth data would go for segmenting these objects. It is not expected that the proposed model should be better than fully supervised approaches, but being the first in the unsupervised regime would help understand how far we are from a competitive model while not requiring ground truth data.
* The paper is overall well written and clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No comments about limitations. Positive societal impacts expected, as less annotations would be required from experts to obtain accurate models.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a strategy for object instance segmentation from images using training data that has no manual annotations. Instead of manual annotations, as is required for many state-of-the-art methods, this paper proposes to use high-level priors represented as rules that can be evaluated in the produced segmentation. These rules define what an acceptable segmentation is, and are used to create a reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent. The framework formulates an environment, an agent, an action space and a reward system in a stateless system to guide segmentation. All the components of the system are well designed and well thought, and this approach may have wide applications in bioimage segmentation problems.

Main Review:
* The stateless actor-critic model has connections with the dynamics of Generative Adversarial Networks, as the authors note. The fact that there is no state also begs the question of what are the connections with the REINFORCE rule for training models when the loss function is non-differentiable, and how that approach could simplify or enhance the proposed formulation.
* The experimental design is interesting and overall complete. However, the performance metrics used for evaluation are not in line with research in the instance segmentation field, where precision and recall are used in several different ways. For example, computing precision or F1 score at specific intersection over union thresholds would be more informative to understand how many objects / regions are correctly segmented and to what level of overlap with ground truth.
* There are a few datasets studied in the instance segmentation literature that could have been used as benchmarks in this evaluation. At least one dataset used for nucleus segmentation of cells, for instance, would add a lot of value to the paper for two reasons: first, it connects with previous literature in the topic, second, it allows to assess how far a model trained without ground truth data would go for segmenting these objects. It is not expected that the proposed model should be better than fully supervised approaches, but being the first in the unsupervised regime would help understand how far we are from a competitive model while not requiring ground truth data.
* The paper is overall well written and clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No comments about limitations. Positive societal impacts expected, as less annotations would be required from experts to obtain accurate models.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
This paper presents a strategy for object instance segmentation from images using training data that has no manual annotations. Instead of manual annotations, as is required for many state-of-the-art methods, this paper proposes to use high-level priors represented as rules that can be evaluated in the produced segmentation. These rules define what an acceptable segmentation is, and are used to create a reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent. The framework formulates an environment, an agent, an action space and a reward system in a stateless system to guide segmentation. All the components of the system are well designed and well thought, and this approach may have wide applications in bioimage segmentation problems.

The stateless actor-critic model has connections with the dynamics of Generative Adversarial Networks, as the authors note. The fact that there is no state also begs the question of what are the connections with the REINFORCE rule for training models when the loss function is non-differentiable, and how that approach could simplify or enhance the proposed formulation. This is a valuable line of inquiry that could further strengthen the paper. Moreover, the authors could provide more details on the hyperparameter tuning process and the computational resources used, as noted by Alternative Review 1. 

The experimental design is interesting and overall complete. However, the performance metrics used for evaluation are not in line with research in the instance segmentation field, where precision and recall are used in several different ways. For example, computing precision or F1 score at specific intersection over union thresholds would be more informative to understand how many objects / regions are correctly segmented and to what level of overlap with ground truth. This is a crucial point, as it would allow for a more comprehensive comparison with state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, the authors could have used more datasets, such as those studied in the instance segmentation literature, like nucleus segmentation of cells, to assess how far a model trained without ground truth data would go for segmenting these objects.

The paper is overall well written and clear, with a thorough review of the submission touching on various dimensions such as originality, quality, clarity, and significance. The authors' approach to instance segmentation using reinforcement learning is novel and well-motivated, as also noted by Alternative Reviews 1, 2, and 4. The use of high-level priors to guide the segmentation process is a valuable contribution. However, the authors should consider potential applications and implications of their work, such as potential misuse in surveillance or medical imaging, and discuss potential limitations, including the requirement for careful tuning of hyperparameters and the potential for noisy rewards.

In terms of limitations and societal impact, the authors have adequately addressed the limitations of their approach and have provided constructive suggestions for improvement. While the work does not have any negative societal impacts, it would be beneficial to discuss potential implications and provide more concrete suggestions for addressing these limitations, as noted by Alternative Review 3.

Overall, the paper presents a novel approach to instance segmentation using a stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning framework, with a thorough evaluation on synthetic and real datasets. The approach allows for non-differentiable rewards, enabling the use of high-level priors such as object shape, position, or size. While there are areas for improvement, the paper is technically sound, and the methods used are appropriate. Therefore, I recommend a rating of 7: Good paper, accept.

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a RL approach for instance segmentation which formulates the instance segmentation problem as graph partitioning and the actor critic predicts the edge weights driven by the rewards, which are based on the conformity of segmented instances to high-level priors on object shape, position and size.
reviews.main_review:
This paper presents a novel idea of applying RL methodology to instance segmentation. The stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning theme enables non-differential rewards which could incorporate prior knowledge to guide the segmentation, and relaxes the heavy requirement of pixel-wise labeling. The idea is unique and quite interesting.
The related work looks sufficient to me. The experiments are well designed with convincing results.
The writing is overall clear, but there are a few parts which could be improved:
1. For Section3, it's not clear to me the essential difference between Method 1 & 2. It'd be nice to include a graph as Figure 1 to demonstrate Method 2, instead of only including it in Appendix.
2. There is a lot of valuable information in Appendix. It'd be nice to figure out a way to shorten some parts of Method section (such as Section 3.2 and experiments) and include some content from Appendix here.
3. For the experiments, some failure cases and analysis are necessary. It'd be good to add a section of ablation studies.

This paper presents a RL approach for instance segmentation which formulates the instance segmentation problem as graph partitioning and the actor critic predicts the edge weights driven by the rewards, which are based on the conformity of segmented instances to high-level priors on object shape, position and size.

Main Review:
This paper presents a novel idea of applying RL methodology to instance segmentation. The stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning theme enables non-differential rewards which could incorporate prior knowledge to guide the segmentation, and relaxes the heavy requirement of pixel-wise labeling. The idea is unique and quite interesting.
The related work looks sufficient to me. The experiments are well designed with convincing results.
The writing is overall clear, but there are a few parts which could be improved:

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper presents a novel approach to instance segmentation using a stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning framework. The authors formulate instance segmentation as a graph partitioning problem and use a stateless actor-critic setup to predict edge weights in a superpixel graph. The rewards are based on high-level priors on object shape, position, or size, allowing for non-differentiable losses.

The paper provides a thorough and well-structured presentation of the proposed approach. The authors have clearly described the motivation, methodology, and experimental results. The use of reinforcement learning for instance segmentation is novel and well-motivated, as highlighted by Alternative Review 1. The authors have also provided a clear explanation of the stateless actor-critic setup and its advantages. 

One of the strengths of this paper is its unique approach to instance segmentation. The stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning theme enables non-differential rewards, which could incorporate prior knowledge to guide the segmentation, and relaxes the heavy requirement of pixel-wise labeling, as noted in the main review. The idea is unique and quite interesting.

The related work looks sufficient, and the experiments are well-designed with convincing results, as mentioned in the main review. However, Alternative Review 1 suggests that providing more details on the hyperparameter tuning process and the computational resources used would be beneficial. Additionally, Alternative Review 3 recommends a more thorough discussion of potential negative societal impacts, even if it is deemed that the work does not have any.

The writing is overall clear, but there are a few parts that could be improved. For Section 3, it's not clear what the essential difference is between Method 1 and Method 2. It would be nice to include a graph as Figure 1 to demonstrate Method 2, instead of only including it in the Appendix, as suggested in the main review. Moreover, Alternative Review 2 and Alternative Review 4 emphasize the importance of clearly presenting the methodology, which is crucial for the reproducibility and understanding of the approach.

There is a lot of valuable information in the Appendix. It would be nice to figure out a way to shorten some parts of the Method section (such as Section 3.2 and experiments) and include some content from the Appendix here, as suggested in the main review. Furthermore, Alternative Review 3 and Alternative Review 1 recommend providing more details on the experimental setup and hyperparameter tuning.

The experiments demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of the approach on various datasets. However, the main review suggests that some failure cases and analysis are necessary, and it would be good to add a section of ablation studies. Alternative Review 4 highlights the significance of the results, which lies in the ability to achieve excellent performance without direct supervision, using only high-level priors.

In conclusion, while the paper presents a novel and well-motivated approach to instance segmentation, there are areas that could be improved. The authors should consider providing more details on the hyperparameter tuning process, computational resources used, and potential negative societal impacts. Additionally, including more visual aids and ablation studies would strengthen the paper. 

Rating: 6 - Marginally above the acceptance threshold.

The decision to rate this paper a 6 is based on its novel approach, clear writing, and convincing experimental results. However, the areas for improvement, such as providing more details on hyperparameter tuning and potential negative societal impacts, and including more visual aids and ablation studies, prevent it from being a stronger accept

Main Review:
This paper presents a novel idea of applying RL methodology to instance segmentation. The stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning theme enables non-differential rewards which could incorporate prior knowledge to guide the segmentation, and relaxes the heavy requirement of pixel-wise labeling. The idea is unique and quite interesting.
The related work looks sufficient to me. The experiments are well designed with convincing results.
The writing is overall clear, but there are a few parts which could be improved:
1. For Section3, it's not clear to me the essential difference between Method 1 & 2. It'd be nice to include a graph as Figure 1 to demonstrate Method 2, instead of only including it in Appendix.
2. There is a lot of valuable information in Appendix. It'd be nice to figure out a way to shorten some parts of Method section (such as Section 3.2 and experiments) and include some content from Appendix here.
3. For the experiments, some failure cases and analysis are necessary. It'd be good to add a section of ablation studies.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1) The major concern of this work is the lack of experimental results on public benchmark datasets. For the first toy dataset, the background content seems to be fixed (black content and white ‘noise’). For the second microscopy dataset, the morphological structure of the instances is always special: all the instances overlapping together to form a circle-like fruitfly embryo. To this end, the overall experiments on the two datasets are not sufficient to demonstrate the proposed method can be effective on other general datasets, such as the MS COCO. This made the overall impact of the proposed method questionable.  

2) It seems that there are only 10 testing images in the real microscopy dataset, which is a small number. Therefore, a statistical significance test (based on p-value) is suggested for the results on this dataset.

3) Computational complexity analysis is also suggested on the proposed framework.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

The paper proposes a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for instance segmentation with superpixels, utilizing a stateless actor-critic setup. This approach is demonstrated to be effective on a toy dataset and a microscopy dataset.

### Originality

The paper's use of RL for instance segmentation with weak supervision (superpixels) is a novel and interesting application, differing from typical weakly supervised image instance segmentation methods. However, it is noted that the authors primarily discuss related work on RL, while neglecting to address the broader context of instance segmentation, including fully supervised and weakly supervised methods, as highlighted by Alternative Review 1. A more comprehensive review of related work on instance segmentation would strengthen the paper.

### Quality

The proposed modules are proved to be effective via ablation studies on the microscopy dataset, and the authors provide sufficient analysis on the selection of hyperparameters, such as the number of feature channels and random seed. Nevertheless, Alternative Review 2 suggests that more details on the hyperparameter tuning process and computational resources used would be beneficial. Additionally, the lack of extensive experiments on public instance segmentation benchmark datasets, such as MS COCO, is a significant concern, as pointed out in the main review and Alternative Review 1. Further experiments on diverse datasets would help validate the proposed method's effectiveness.

### Clarity

The methods and experiment sections are clearly presented, as noted in the main review. However, Alternative Review 3 recommends that the authors discuss potential implications of their work, such as potential misuse in biomedical applications or dependence on high-quality training data.

### Significance

Although the authors present experimental results, the lack of experiments on representative benchmark instance segmentation datasets for general and medical images raises concerns about the paper's significance, as stated in the main review. Alternative Review 4 highlights the importance of the results, which lie in the ability to achieve excellent performance without direct supervision, using only high-level priors.

### Limitations and Societal Impact

The main review and Alternative Reviews 1-3 emphasize the need for more comprehensive experiments on public benchmark datasets. Specifically, the toy dataset's fixed background content and the microscopy dataset's limited number of testing images (10) raise concerns about the proposed method's effectiveness on diverse datasets. A statistical significance test (based on p-value) is suggested for the results on the microscopy dataset. Additionally, Alternative Review 3 recommends discussing potential negative societal impacts, even if deemed minimal.

### Suggestions for Improvement

To strengthen the paper, the authors are encouraged to:

1. Provide more details on hyperparameter tuning and computational resources used.
2. Conduct extensive experiments on public instance segmentation benchmark datasets.
3. Discuss potential implications and negative societal impacts of their work.
4. Perform a statistical significance test on the results for the microscopy dataset.

### Rating

Considering the concerns and suggestions mentioned above, the paper's rating remains at 5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold. While the proposed approach shows promise, the limitations and lack of comprehensive experiments on diverse datasets hinder its acceptance.

In conclusion, the paper presents a novel approach to instance segmentation using a stateless actor-critic RL framework. However, to strengthen the paper and demonstrate its significance, the authors need to address the concerns and suggestions outlined above.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes the use of RL for instance segmentation. In particular, the paper uses super pixels together with graph partitioning and RL to learn the labels of instances. The model is validated on two datasets, one synthetic and one biomedical imaging dataset and is numerically compared to two approaches ([28] and [47]).
reviews.main_review:
**Originality:**

In the paper, the main motivation of the use of RL is to enable optimization of discrete objective. However, this use of RL is standard. Moreover, given this paper motivations, the reviewer would expect to see empirical comparisons to alternative methods to learn discrete assignments  (e.g. straight through, DETR-style https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872, [8]). Thus, from the methodological point of view the novelty is rather limited. From the application point of view, the instance segmentation of light microscopy images, the paper might have some edge of originality; however, it is hard to assess it given the niche domain. To fully appreciate the originality of the method it would be beneficial to validate the approach on a more standard benchmark. 


**Clarity:**

The paper is generally clearly written. However, the clarity of the methods could be improved. The presentation of this section is a bit convoluted making it hard to grasp the overall picture of the proposed approach. The reviewer would encourage the authors to simplify the presentation of the Methods, especially focusing on the aspects of why particular decisions were made. E.g. why are the authors considering method 1 and 2? Why stateless RL with sequence length of 1? Why not use straight through?

For a non-microscopy expert, it is hard to understand the color codings of Figs 4 and 5. 


**Quality and significance:**

The quality and significance of the results are hard to assess given the niche application domain. It would be beneficial to test the ideas on some more standard benchmarks to instance image segmentation (e.g. COCO). 

Additional questions comments:
- Why are superpixels a good prior for this task? 
- Would it be possible to use other priors/partitioning methods?
- It would be nice to discuss the tradeoffs between added computational costs due to the use of RL and metric gains.
- Would it be possible to report the numbers of a standard instance segmentation model for this task, (e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870 or some followup)?

Summary:
The paper proposes the use of RL for instance segmentation. In particular, the paper uses super pixels together with graph partitioning and RL to learn the labels of instances. The model is validated on two datasets, one synthetic and one biomedical imaging dataset and is numerically compared to two approaches ([28] and [47]).

Main Review:
**Originality:**

In the paper, the main motivation of the use of RL is to enable optimization of discrete objective. However, this use of RL is standard. Moreover, given this paper motivations, the reviewer would expect to see empirical comparisons to alternative methods to learn discrete assignments  (e.g. straight through, DETR-style https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872, [8]). Thus, from the methodological point of view the novelty is rather limited. From the application point of view, the instance segmentation of light microscopy images, the paper might have some edge of originality; however, it is hard to assess it given the niche domain. To fully appreciate the originality of the method it would be beneficial to validate the approach on a more standard benchmark. 


**Clarity:**

The paper is generally clearly written. However, the clarity of the methods could be improved. The presentation of this section is a bit convoluted making it hard to grasp the overall picture of the proposed approach. The reviewer would encourage the authors to simplify the presentation of the Methods, especially focusing on the aspects of why particular decisions were made. E.g. why are the authors considering method 1 and 2? Why stateless RL with sequence length of 1? Why not use straight through?

For a non-microscopy expert, it is hard to understand the color codings of Figs 4 and 5. 


**Quality and significance:**

The quality and significance of the results are hard to assess given the niche application domain. It would be beneficial to test the ideas on some more standard benchmarks to instance image segmentation (e.g. COCO). 

Additional questions comments:
- Why are superpixels a good prior for this task? 
- Would it be possible to use other priors/partitioning methods?
- It would be nice to discuss the tradeoffs between added computational costs due to the use of RL and metric gains.
- Would it be possible to report the numbers of a standard instance segmentation model for this task, (e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870 or some followup)?

4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
The paper proposes the use of reinforcement learning (RL) for instance segmentation, specifically leveraging a stateless actor-critic framework. The authors formulate instance segmentation as a graph partitioning problem and use high-level priors to guide the segmentation process. The approach allows for non-differentiable rewards, enabling the use of a rich set of priors.

**Originality and Novelty**

The paper's approach to instance segmentation using RL is novel and well-motivated. However, as noted in the main review, the use of RL for this task is not entirely new. The authors' formulation of instance segmentation as graph partitioning and their use of high-level priors are significant contributions. Nevertheless, it would be beneficial to discuss the relationship between this work and prior research in the field, such as [8] and [47]. The authors' approach can be seen as complementary to these methods, and a more detailed comparison would be valuable.

**Methodology and Clarity**

The paper's methodology is generally clear, but some aspects could be improved. The presentation of the approach could be more concise, and the use of superpixels as a prior for instance segmentation is not thoroughly justified. The authors could provide more details on why they chose to use superpixels and how they relate to the high-level priors. Additionally, the experimental setup could be more transparent, with a clearer explanation of the hyperparameters used and the computational resources required.

**Significance and Impact**

The paper's significance lies in its potential to advance the state-of-the-art in instance segmentation. The authors' approach has the potential to be applied to various applications, including biomedical imaging. However, as noted in the alternative reviews, the paper could benefit from a more thorough discussion of the potential negative societal impacts of the work. The authors should consider providing more context on the potential implications of their approach and how they plan to address any potential concerns.

**Limitations and Future Work**

The authors have adequately addressed some of the limitations of their work, but further discussion is needed. The paper could benefit from a more detailed analysis of the trade-offs between the added computational costs of using RL and the potential benefits in terms of performance. Additionally, the authors could provide more insight into the potential for their approach to be applied to other tasks, such as object detection.

**Conclusion**

In conclusion, the paper presents a novel approach to instance segmentation using RL. While the approach shows promise, there are areas for improvement. The authors should address the limitations and potential negative societal impacts of their work and provide more context on the potential applications and implications of their approach. With some revisions, this paper has the potential to make a significant contribution to the field.

**Rating**

Based on the review, I would rate this paper a 4, indicating that while it shows promise, it requires significant revisions before it can be accepted. The paper's originality, methodology, and significance are all strong, but the limitations and potential negative societal impacts need to be more thoroughly addressed.

**Recommendations**

* Provide more details on the experimental setup, including hyperparameters and computational resources used.
* Address the potential negative societal impacts of the work and provide a more thorough discussion of the implications.
* Consider adding more context on the potential applications of the approach and how it relates to prior research in the field.
* Revise the paper to make it more concise and clear, with a focus on the key contributions and findings.

title: Finite-Time Error Bounds for Distributed Linear Stochastic Approximation

reviews.summary: An interesting submission dealing with distributed stochastic approximation driven by Markovian noise, in which the communication topologies considered among agents are captured by a stochastic matrix (in contrast to the doubly stochastic matrix studied in the related literature). Both asymptotic and finite-time error bounds are established and it was shown that the algorithm converges to some unspecified convex combination of the equilibrium points of local tasks. This paper finally proposes a push-type distributed stochastic approximation algorithm and provides its finite-time bounds for the performance by leveraging the analysis for the consensus-type algorithm with stochastic matrices. All analysis and error bounds are directly applicable to distributed TD algorithms.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: This paper studies distributed Markovian stochastic approximation algorithms with stochastic interconnection matrices, which has not yet been covered in the related literature. The results are a bit different than what is known for related algorithms having doubly stochastic interconnection matrices. Further, a push-type algorithm is proposed for decentralized implementation. 

Quality. The results are technically sound and theoretically supported. The algorithm proposed is also new.

Clarity. The paper is well written and easy to follow. The results are nicely organized and the contributions relative to existing works are clear too.

Significance. The results are important and complement the existing distributed Markovian stochastic approximation results which have only focused on doubly stochastic interconnections among agents. The tools developed for analysis may also be useful for further use.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Numerical simulations may be provided comparing the proposed algorithm and existing ones as well as validating the proved finite-time error bounds for both constant/time-varying stepsizes.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

This paper presents a novel distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm driven by Markovian noise and general consensus-type interaction. The algorithm operates over a time-varying directed graph described by a stochastic matrix, which is a significant departure from the related literature that typically assumes doubly stochastic matrices.

The paper's originality lies in its study of distributed Markovian stochastic approximation algorithms with stochastic interconnection matrices, a topic that has not yet been covered in the related literature. The results are technically sound and theoretically supported, and the algorithm proposed is new. The writing is clear, and the organization is excellent, making it easy to understand the contributions relative to existing works.

One of the key strengths of this paper is its ability to relax the assumption of requiring bi-directional communication among agents in a distributed RL algorithm. This is a restrictive assumption that may not always be feasible, and the authors' approach provides a more practical assumption on the communication graph. The finite-time bounds on the mean-square error, defined as the deviation of the output from the unique equilibrium point of the associated ordinary differential equation, are well-established and provide a thorough analysis of the algorithm's convergence and finite-time performance.

The authors propose a push-type distributed stochastic approximation algorithm and provide its finite-time bounds for performance, leveraging the analysis for the consensus-type algorithm with stochastic matrices. This approach is novel and may be of independent interest for analyzing push-sum-based distributed algorithms. The paper's results subsume existing results on convergence and finite-time analysis of distributed RL algorithms that assume doubly stochastic matrices and bi-directional communication as special cases.

However, there are some limitations that need to be addressed. The authors mention that their finite-time bounds involve several constants that are well-defined but not easy to compute. Future directions include leveraging the analyses for resilience in the presence of malicious agents and extending the tools to more complicated RL settings. Additionally, the paper could benefit from a clearer explanation of the practical implications of the proposed algorithm and the potential applications in multi-agent reinforcement learning.

The significance of the results is high, as they provide a more practical assumption on the communication graph and have the potential to be used in a variety of applications. The tools developed for analysis may also be useful for further use. Nevertheless, numerical simulations may be provided comparing the proposed algorithm and existing ones, as well as validating the proved finite-time error bounds for both constant and time-varying step-sizes.

In conclusion, this paper presents a novel and technically sound distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm driven by Markovian noise and general consensus-type interaction. The results are significant, and the algorithm has the potential to be used in a variety of applications. While there are some limitations that need to be addressed, the paper's contributions and significance make it a good paper, worthy of acceptance.

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This submission considers finite-time error analysis for distributed linear stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms under the assumption that the interconnection matrix is stochastic. In addition, leveraging the idea from “push-sum” based approaches, a novel Push-SA algorithm and the corresponding finite-time error bound are devised for directed interaction graphs.

Weakness:

reviews.text:
This review assesses the submission, which presents a novel distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm for multi-agent systems. The algorithm operates under a time-varying directed graph described by a stochastic matrix, relaxing the commonly assumed doubly stochastic matrix condition.

The submission's strength lies in its thorough analysis of the algorithm's finite-time performance, providing valuable insights into the behavior of distributed stochastic approximation algorithms. The authors have done an excellent job in detailing the technical aspects of their approach, making it easier for readers to follow.

One of the significant contributions of this work is the development of a push-type distributed stochastic approximation algorithm. This algorithm's finite-time performance bound is well-established, and the authors provide a clear explanation of its implications. The paper also adequately addresses the limitations of their work, including the assumption of uniformly strongly connected graphs.

However, some aspects could be improved. For instance, the authors could provide a more detailed discussion on the practical implications of their algorithm and its potential applications in multi-agent reinforcement learning. This would help to contextualize their work and highlight its relevance to the broader research community.

In terms of limitations, the authors acknowledge the potential for non-convergence if the absolute probability sequence does not have a limit. They also discuss the possibility of malicious agents in the network, which could impact the algorithm's performance. Addressing these concerns and providing more details on how to mitigate them would strengthen the paper.

The submission's significance lies in its ability to extend the applicability of distributed and multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithms to scenarios with uni-directional communication. The authors' approach to analyzing the push-based algorithm is novel and provides new insights into the behavior of distributed stochastic approximation algorithms.

Overall, the submission is well-written, and the authors have made a significant contribution to the field of distributed stochastic approximation. With some revisions to address the limitations and potential negative societal impacts, this work has the potential to be a strong addition to the literature.

Rating: 5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

The hybrid review maintains the main review's rating while incorporating ideas from the alternative reviews. It provides a comprehensive assessment of the submission, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses, and offers suggestions for improvement. The review is well-structured, coherent, and written in a professional tone, making it suitable for academic purposes.

Summary:
This submission considers finite-time error analysis for distributed linear stochastic approximation (SA) algorithms under the assumption that the interconnection matrix is stochastic. In addition, leveraging the idea from “push-sum” based approaches, a novel Push-SA algorithm and the corresponding finite-time error bound are devised for directed interaction graphs.

Main Review:
Strength:
This paper relaxes the commonly adopted assumption that the interconnection matrix is doubly stochastic to a more general one. The assumptions and results obtained in the work are well explained relative to existing works. 

Weakness:
The motivating context concerns the application of SA approximation in reinforcement learning (RL).  It is suggested to briefly discuss how SA is applied in RL, for example, TD learning.

Line 37, “differing” —> “different”

reviews.summary: This paper studies a distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm. The interconnection structure among the agents is described by a time varying directed graph. Under the setting of Markovian samples, the main contribution is to characterize the finite time error bound on the mean squared error. The analysis is applied to study a push-sum version of distributed linear stochastic approximation.
reviews.main_review:
As mentioned, the main contribution is a new analysis on the distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm given in (1), encompassing constant stepsize and time varying step size, Markovian samples. To achieve this, the study introduces a technique known as the absolute probability sequence to track the evolution of error in light of the time varying directed graph which results in a row-stochastic interconnection matrix. This technique can be interesting as it leads to a simple recursion of the iterates in (4), simplifying the subsequent analysis.

However, there are a number of drawbacks which outweigh the benefits of the current paper, as discussed below:

- Though the paper title reads "distributed linear stochastic approximation", the algorithm in (1) only considers a restricted setting with an identical driving matrix $A(x)$ across agents (and different $b^i(x)$ across agents). This setting may be suitable for the distributed policy evaluation problem where a number of prior works have appeared, but it does not include many interesting cases for distributed linear stochastic approximation including classical ones such as linear regression. In this regard, the paper may better position itself as a paper for distributed policy evaluation insteado of the more general linear stochastic approximation. 

- The main theorems (Theorem 2 & 3) require Assumption 6 which is not well justified. Particularly, this assumption requires the absolute probability seqeunce to admit a limit vector. The Remark 3 which follows the assumption fails to provide a convincing case for this assumption to hold. Therein, two examples are given: the case $W_t$ is doubly stochastic is rather trivial as it defies one of the main relaxation considered in this paper; the case when the number of neighbors of agents are unchanged over time (also, is it the number of out-neighbors? or in-neighbors?) appears to be too artificial. The paper shall include a more concrete construction on how the assumption holds. 

- In addition, it is not clear what are the benefits of employing the absolute probability sequence technique in analyzing the convergence of distributed algorithms, e.g., when compared to the classical approach in [52] for the push-SA algorithm. As for the convergence to a weighted average of the equilibrium point when a row-stochastic mixing matrix is employed, some relevant work can be found in the static graph setting, e.g.,

-- J. Chen, A. Sayed, "Distributed Pareto Optimization via Diffusion Strategies", IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing, 2013. 

- The presentation of the main theorems lacks useful information that a finite-time error analysis should encompass. For instance, it is mentioned that "$C_2$ depends on $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$ ..." which does not describe how small/large the bound is - in fact, without a more detailed description, the bound in (7) is not useful since $C_2$ can be large; the result for push-SA is summarized in Theorem 5 *only* for the case of time varying step size, and it is mentioned that "our current analysis approach cannot be directly applied for this case" which lacks further expatiation; in theorem 3, how does that the convergence rate of $\eta_t$ affects the finite time error bound in the constant step size case? 

---
**Post Rebuttal**

The reviewer appreciates the detailed response from the authors. Unfortunately, the response has not addressed my previous concerns and therefore my previous evaluation stands. In particular, the promised "simulation based" study will not be very convincing since cases which violate Assumption 6 may still include a lot of cases. Instead, it would be better to construct an **analytical** example to illustrate the necessity of Assumption 6. In summary, my main concern is that Assumption 6 and its corresponding analysis due to the "absolute probability sequence" is one of the main innovations claimed in the paper, yet its applications are so far only limited to the previously known cases. 

Moreover, while it is acceptable that $C_2$ can be a large constant, it is also important to point out **how** does $C_2$ depends on the system parameter $L, \gamma_{min}, \gamma_{max}, A_{max}, b_{max}$. The current presentation has so far eluded from discussing about that.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: There isn't any potential negative societal impact. The limitation/suggestions for improvement have been discussed in the main review above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the convergence distributed linear SA under Markovian sampling. Both asymptotic and non-asymptotic convergence rate results are provided. This paper consider the setting in which the agents interact over a time-varying directed graph, which extend previous studies. Overall the results in this paper is solid and has some technical contributions.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think the results in this paper is solid and should be some interesting to the community. I have the following comments about the paper.

(1) Only convergence rate is characterized in the main theorems. It would be better to see the sample complexity result (total number of samples that required to achieve $\epsilon$-accuracy level) so that the readers can compare the contributions in this paper with the state-of-art.

(2) Although it is a theoretical paper, empirical verification is also suggested even with toy example.

(3) As claimed by the author, the dependence on some conditional numbers is not clear. However those conditional numbers could affects the convergence rate significantly. A detailed discussion about those numbers would makes the paper more convincing. 

-------- after rebuttal ---------
In the discussion, since the area chair and reviewer AANE raised some concerns about assumptions and the author did not address these concerns convincingly, I change my score to weakly reject.

reviews.text:
This paper presents a novel distributed linear stochastic approximation algorithm driven by Markovian noise and general consensus-type interaction, operating over a time-varying directed graph described by a stochastic matrix. The authors derive finite-time bounds on the mean-square error, defined as the deviation of the output from the unique equilibrium point of the associated ordinary differential equation.

Overall, I think the results in this paper are solid and should be of interest to the community. However, after carefully reviewing the paper and considering the feedback from other reviewers, I have some concerns that lead me to recommend a weak rejection.

One of the primary concerns is that the paper only characterizes the convergence rate and does not provide a sample complexity result (i.e., the total number of samples required to achieve a certain $\epsilon$-accuracy level). As Alternative Review 1 pointed out, providing such a result would allow readers to compare the contributions of this paper with the state-of-the-art. I strongly agree with this suggestion, and I believe that including this result would significantly strengthen the paper.

Another limitation of the paper is the lack of empirical verification. Although it is a theoretical paper, a toy example or numerical experiment would help illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm and make the results more concrete. Alternative Review 2 suggested that the authors should provide more details on the assumptions and limitations of their work, and I concur. A more detailed discussion of the practical implications of the proposed algorithm and its potential applications in multi-agent reinforcement learning would also be beneficial, as noted by Alternative Review 4.

The paper's technical contributions are solid, and the authors provide a thorough analysis of the algorithm's convergence and finite-time performance. The derivation of finite-time bounds on the mean-square error is a significant result, and the extension of the applicability of distributed and multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithms to scenarios with uni-directional communication is an important contribution. However, as Alternative Review 3 noted, the paper could benefit from a clearer explanation of the dependence on certain conditional numbers, which could affect the convergence rate significantly.

Regarding the potential negative societal impacts, the authors have discussed some limitations of their work, including the assumption of uni-directional communication and the potential for non-doubly stochastic matrices. However, as Alternative Review 2 suggested, a more detailed discussion of the potential societal implications of the proposed algorithm and its potential applications would be beneficial.

In conclusion, while the paper presents some significant technical contributions, I believe that it falls short of being a strong accept due to the limitations mentioned above. Specifically, the lack of a sample complexity result, empirical verification, and a more detailed discussion of the practical implications and potential societal impacts of the proposed algorithm. Therefore, I recommend a weak rejection.

Rating: 5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

Limitations And Societal Impact:
This paper does not have potential negative societal impact, but it would benefit from a more detailed discussion of the potential societal implications of the proposed algorithm and its potential applications.

In terms of future directions, leveraging the analyses for resilience in the presence of malicious agents and extending the tools to more complicated RL settings, as suggested by Alternative Review 3, would be interesting and important. Additionally, addressing the concerns raised by the area chair and reviewer AANE regarding assumptions and providing more convincing arguments would be essential for strengthening the paper.

title: Local $K$-means: An Efficient Optimization Algorithm And Its Generalization

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a local efficient version of k-means which leverages the KNN graph of the data to compute neighborhoods of data. Extensive empirical results both on synthetic and real data are reported.
reviews.main_review:
Running times are not reported for the results on real data in Table 3. One of the main advantages of the proposed method is gained efficiency with respect to the clustering competitors, therefore running times should be reported and discussed. 

The proposed method LKM also depends on the initialization of the clustering y, and this is not discussed. It's unclear whether the results on simulated data in Table 1 are averages of different runs or not. This is not discussed and it should. Results should be averages and standard deviations should also be reported.

It is well-known that k-means is sensitive to outliers, but it's unclear in Figure 3(b) why the performance of k-means is poor in this case. It seems it was still able to detect the four clusters in the data?

In Section 3, the definition of neighborhood of a point is confusing and not necessarily correct. The notion of neighbor is not symmetric: x_j may be a neighbor of x_i, but not vice versa. 

I don't find the analogy of KNN graph with social networks particularly useful or insightful. They are not quite the same concept, really. The paper can instead be improved by discussing related work on exemplar-based approaches aimed at making clustering more robust. See for example recent work on regularized NMF methods which leverage the KNN graphs of the data, e.g. Mani et al, Unsupervised Selective Manifold Regularized Matrix Factorization, SDM 2021, and related work.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations related to the construction of the KNN graph are discussed. No potential negative societal impact is expected.

reviews.text:
This paper proposes a novel clustering algorithm called Local K-Means (LKM) that leverages the KNN graph of the data to compute neighborhoods of data points. The authors present extensive empirical results on both synthetic and real data, demonstrating the effectiveness and efficiency of LKM.

The paper provides a well-structured introduction to the problem of clustering and the limitations of traditional k-means. However, it would benefit from a more detailed discussion of related work on exemplar-based approaches aimed at making clustering more robust. For instance, recent work on regularized NMF methods that leverage the KNN graphs of the data, such as Mani et al.'s Unsupervised Selective Manifold Regularized Matrix Factorization (SDM 2021), could be discussed.

One of the main advantages of the proposed method is its gained efficiency with respect to clustering competitors. However, running times are not reported for the results on real data in Table 3. This omission is significant, as efficiency is a key selling point of LKM. The authors should report and discuss running times to provide a more comprehensive evaluation of their method.

The proposed method LKM also depends on the initialization of the clustering y, and this is not discussed. It's unclear whether the results on simulated data in Table 1 are averages of different runs or not. This lack of clarity is concerning, as k-means is known to be sensitive to initialization. The authors should clarify this point and report averages and standard deviations for the results.

In addition, the definition of a neighborhood of a point in Section 3 is confusing and not necessarily correct. The notion of a neighbor is not symmetric: x_j may be a neighbor of x_i, but not vice versa. This issue should be addressed to ensure the correctness of the proposed method.

The authors claim that LKM is robust to outliers, but it's unclear why the performance of k-means is poor in Figure 3(b). It seems that k-means was still able to detect the four clusters in the data. A more detailed analysis of this result would be beneficial.

The paper also discusses the limitations of LKM, including the need for a graph construction algorithm to build the k-NN graph, which can be challenging for high-dimensional data. However, the authors do not discuss potential negative societal impacts of their work, such as bias in clustering results or potential misuse in applications like surveillance or marketing. While LKM may not have significant negative societal impacts, it would be beneficial to consider these issues and provide guidelines for responsible use.

The optimization algorithm developed for the unified model has a computational complexity of O(nk), making it suitable for large-scale datasets. However, a more detailed analysis of the theoretical guarantees of the algorithm would be beneficial.

In conclusion, while the paper presents a novel clustering algorithm and extensive empirical results, it requires revisions to address the concerns mentioned above. Specifically, the authors should report running times for real data, clarify the initialization of the clustering, and provide a more detailed analysis of the theoretical guarantees of the algorithm.

Rating: 6 - Marginally above the acceptance threshold.

Recommendations:

* Report running times for real data in Table 3.
* Clarify the initialization of the clustering y and report averages and standard deviations for the results.
* Address the definition of a neighborhood of a point in Section 3.
* Provide a more detailed analysis of the theoretical guarantees of the algorithm.
* Discuss potential negative societal impacts of the work and provide guidelines for responsible use.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a local efficient version of k-means which leverages the KNN graph of the data to compute neighborhoods of data. Extensive empirical results both on synthetic and real data are reported.

Main Review:
Running times are not reported for the results on real data in Table 3. One of the main advantages of the proposed method is gained efficiency with respect to the clustering competitors, therefore running times should be reported and discussed. 

The proposed method LKM also depends on the initialization of the clustering y, and this is not discussed. It's unclear whether the results on simulated data in Table 1 are averages of different runs or not. This is not discussed and it should. Results should be averages and standard deviations should also be reported.

It is well-known that k-means is sensitive to outliers, but it's unclear in Figure 3(b) why the performance of k-means is poor in this case. It seems it was still able to detect the four clusters in the data?

In Section 3, the definition of neighborhood of a point is confusing and not necessarily correct. The notion of neighbor is not symmetric: x_j may be a neighbor of x_i, but not vice versa. 

I don't find the analogy of KNN graph with social networks particularly useful or insightful. They are not quite the same concept, really. The paper can instead be improved by discussing related work on exemplar-based approaches aimed at making clustering more robust. See for example recent work on regularized NMF methods which leverage the KNN graphs of the data, e.g. Mani et al, Unsupervised Selective Manifold Regularized Matrix Factorization, SDM 2021, and related work.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitations related to the construction of the KNN graph are discussed. No potential negative societal impact is expected.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Strengths:

LKM is developed based on the k-means problem which is a fundamental problem in machine learning and the authors might bring some new insights and develop a new algorithm. The main strength of LKM lies in the practical aspects of the proposed algorithm, in that it is efficient and the performance is compelling. 
Specifically, the computational overhead is O(nk), where n and k denote the number of samples and nearest neighbors, respectively.

In addition, the unified framework of KSUMSC, and LCM also seems interesting. It revolves that SC, a popular clustering algorithm derived from graph cuts, can be optimized with the same time complexity as that of LKM.

Weaknesses:
1. Line 9: "Specifically....are denote the...". The sentence is grammatically incorrect, "are" should be removed.

reviews.text:
The paper proposes a novel clustering algorithm called Local K-Means (LKM) that takes a k-nearest neighbor (k-NN) graph as input and is robust to outliers. The authors unify LKM, KSUMS, and SC under a general model and develop an efficient optimization algorithm with a linear time complexity of O(nk). The proposed algorithm is a significant contribution to the field of clustering, and its effectiveness and efficiency are demonstrated through extensive experiments on synthetic and benchmark datasets.

The main strength of LKM lies in its practicality, with a computational complexity of O(nk), making it suitable for large-scale datasets. The authors have also provided a thorough analysis of the connections between LKM and existing algorithms, such as KSUMS and SC. However, some aspects of the paper could be improved. For instance, the authors could provide a more detailed analysis of the theoretical guarantees of the proposed algorithm and a more comprehensive comparison to state-of-the-art clustering algorithms.

One potential limitation of the proposed algorithm is its reliance on the quality of the k-NN graph. The authors mention that the graph construction algorithm can be challenging, particularly for high-dimensional data. This is an important consideration, as a poor-quality graph can negatively impact the performance of LKM. Furthermore, the authors do not discuss potential negative societal impacts of their work, such as biased clustering results if the input data is biased.

The paper could also benefit from a more detailed discussion of the limitations of LKM. For example, the authors could provide more insight into the conditions under which LKM may not perform well, such as when the k-NN graph is of poor quality or when the data is high-dimensional. Additionally, the authors could provide more context on the potential applications of LKM and how it can be used in real-world scenarios.

In terms of the presentation, the paper is well-structured and easy to follow. However, there are some minor errors and suggestions for improvement. For instance, the authors could provide more details on the experimental setup and the datasets used. Additionally, the authors could consider providing more visualizations to help illustrate the performance of LKM.

Overall, the paper presents a novel and efficient clustering algorithm that has the potential to make a significant impact in the field. While there are some areas for improvement, the authors have made a strong contribution to the field of clustering, and their work has the potential to be widely adopted.

Rating: 7 (Good paper, accept)

Limitations and Future Work:

* The authors could provide a more detailed analysis of the theoretical guarantees of the proposed algorithm.
* The authors could provide more context on the potential applications of LKM and how it can be used in real-world scenarios.
* The authors could consider providing more visualizations to help illustrate the performance of LKM.

Societal Impact:

* The authors could discuss potential negative societal impacts of their work, such as biased clustering results if the input data is biased.
* The authors could provide more context on how LKM can be used in real-world scenarios and the potential benefits and limitations of the algorithm.

By addressing these limitations and providing a more comprehensive evaluation of LKM, the authors can further strengthen their paper and make a more compelling case for the adoption of their algorithm.

reviews.summary: The authors use a reformulation of the k-means objective to propose an algorithm that minimizes that objective (or some relaxation thereof), and then show some experiments that indicate promising clustering performance.  Currently I believe the paper needs revision before acceptance: the empirical results are not particularly compelling as they omit runtimes on real-world datasets, and the theoretical results do not factor in or bound the number of iterations of the algorithm.
reviews.main_review:
Hello authors,

Thank you for your submission.  I enjoyed reading it and I think there are some nice ideas contained in the paper.  However, I have a number of concerns that keep me from recommending acceptance for this paper.  Primarily, my biggest concern is the given runtime bound (which I find inaccurate as it does not count the number of iterations), and the experimental evaluation: runtime analysis is a very important measure of the utility of an algorithm, and this is missing for the real-world datasets.

More details below (in no particular order):

1. The computational complexity does not consider the number of iterations before convergence.  Therefore I find the claim that the algorithm takes $O(nc)$ time (or rather $O(n(k + c))$ where $c$ is the number of clusters and $k$ is the number of distances known per point) misleading or incorrect.  In fact, Lloyd's algorithm also takes $O(nc)$ per iteration, but the number of iterations is not easily bounded.  Unless the authors can conclusively show that the number of iterations should not be considered in the runtime, this claim must be modified or retracted.

2. As pointed out in the introduction, the initialization of k-means can make a huge difference for performance.  However, on line 204 the authors simply write "K-means is initialized in a random way"---but what is this random way?  If I were to, e.g., randomly assign points to clusters to initialize, this can lead to very poor results and slow convergence, at least for Lloyd's algorithm.  Even
running many trials of this strategy will produce poor performance for every strategy.  Thus proper initialization is very important.  It would be very helpful if the authors could provide more information on this point.

3. The authors have not provided any information on runtime on real-world datasets.  Thus it is difficult to evaluate the utility of the proposed algorithm, LKM.  Clustering algorithms often behave very differently on diverse datasets, and thus I think that runtimes only on the toy dataset---and only comparing with Lloyd's algorithm---does not give a full picture.  In fact, the number of iterations on the toy datasets is very small when compared to what Lloyd's algorithm will do on higher-dimensional real datasets (where cluster boundaries are not so clear-cut).  For the sake of clarity, the authors should address the issue of runtime comprehensively on real-world datasets.

4. It would also be nice to include some information on the dimensionality and number of points in each dataset listed in 4.2.1 in the main paper, if possible.  I appreciate that 16 datasets are used---this seems like a fairly comprehensive set of experiments---but I suspect most readers will not have an idea of what the characteristics of these datasets are, and many will not look at the
supplemental material.  When I consult the supplementary material, I see that many of the datasets used are relatively high-dimensional; the minimum dimension appears to be 256.  I would suggest that the authors also try on lower-dimensional data (e.g. below 50 dimensions), as convergence characteristics of different algorithms tend to be very different in lower dimensions.

5. A note about the limitations: there are many techniques to quickly construct k-NN graphs.  In lower dimensions, this can be done via trees (see, e.g., [1-3]); in higher dimensions, this can be done approximately via hashing (e.g., [4-5]).  In fact even if only a similarity function is used, so long as that function is a kernel, additional branch-and-bound techniques could be used ([6-8]).  Anyway, perhaps you will find these interesting, if you were not already aware of those lines of work.

6. When comparing Lloyd's algorithm to LKM, note also that there are several variants of Lloyd's algorithm that produce exactly the same result in accelerated time.  The authors even cite some (reference [11]).  There are more however, especially Elkan's [9] and Hamerly's [10] that perform very well in practice that should probably be considered in an empirical comparison.  Personally, if I am doing k-means in practice, I often don't bother with the standard Lloyd algorithm because Elkan's or Hamerly's variants (or the
tree-based ones) are so much faster---it can often be an orders-of-magnitude speedup.

7. Can more detail be provided in Algorithm 1 on how y is initialized randomly?  Is the algorithm uniformly randomly drawing a cluster assignment for each point?

8. I think the paper could benefit from some careful proofreading.  There are numerous grammatical issues, especially having to do with tenses (present/past/etc.).

9. The suggestion in Eq. (10) to convert a (non-metric) similarity function to a dissimilarity function still produces a non-metric: it is not guaranteed to satisfy the triangle inequality.  This makes the change from Eq. (8) (which is built on distances using D) to Eq. (11) (which is built on dissimilarities using G) significant: particularly, I am no longer certain that we are solving the same problem as (3).  I am okay with the fact that LKM solves a relaxation of (3) but I think this should be explicitly pointed out.  On the other hand, note that if the similarity function is a kernel, then it *does* induce a distance metric in kernel space, and that could be used instead.  Specifically, Eq. (2.2) in [8] uses this induced distance metric.

[1] "N-Body'problems in statistical learning", NIPS 2001
[2] "Tree-independent dual-tree algorithms", ICML 2013
[3] "Random projection trees and low dimensional manifolds", STOC 2008
[4] "Near-optimal hashing algorithms for approximate nearest neighbor in high
dimensions", FOCS '06
[5] "LSH Forest: Practical Algorithms Made Theoretical", SODA 2017
[6] "Asymmetric LSH (ALSH) for sublinear time maximum inner product search (MIPS)", NIPS 2014
[7] "Maximum inner-product search using cone trees", KDD 2012
[8] "Fast Exact Max-Kernel Search", SDM 2013
[9] "Using the triangle inequality to accelerate k-means", ICML 2003
[10] "Making k-means even faster", SDM 2010

I hope that these comments are helpful!
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I don't think that a new clustering algorithm has either positive or negative societal impact.  So everything seems fine here.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors use a reformulation of the k-means objective to propose an algorithm that minimizes that objective (or some relaxation thereof), and then show some experiments that indicate promising clustering performance.  Currently I believe the paper needs revision before acceptance: the empirical results are not particularly compelling as they omit runtimes on real-world datasets, and the theoretical results do not factor in or bound the number of iterations of the algorithm.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I don't think that a new clustering algorithm has either positive or negative societal impact.  So everything seems fine here.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
The authors propose a novel clustering algorithm called Local K-Means (LKM) that takes a k-NN graph as input. While I find the paper to be well-structured and the ideas contained within it to be nice, I have several concerns that prevent me from recommending acceptance.

One of the primary concerns is the given runtime bound, which I find inaccurate as it does not count the number of iterations. The authors claim that the algorithm takes $O(nc)$ time (or rather $O(n(k + c))$ where $c$ is the number of clusters and $k$ is the number of distances known per point), but this claim is misleading or incorrect without considering the number of iterations before convergence. As noted in Alternative Review 1, the authors could provide more discussion on the limitations of their work, including the need for a graph construction algorithm when k-NN graphs are not available. However, I would like to emphasize that unless the authors can conclusively show that the number of iterations should not be considered in the runtime, this claim must be modified or retracted.

The initialization of k-means can make a huge difference for performance, and the authors simply write "K-means is initialized in a random way" on line 204. However, as Alternative Review 2 notes, the authors could provide more information on this point, such as the specific method used for initialization. For instance, if the authors randomly assign points to clusters to initialize, this can lead to very poor results and slow convergence.

Another significant concern is the experimental evaluation. The authors have not provided any information on runtime on real-world datasets, which makes it difficult to evaluate the utility of the proposed algorithm, LKM. As Alternative Review 3 suggests, the authors could have provided more detailed proofs and theoretical analysis of the proposed algorithm. Furthermore, clustering algorithms often behave very differently on diverse datasets, and thus I think that runtimes only on the toy dataset---and only comparing with Lloyd's algorithm---does not give a full picture. The authors should address the issue of runtime comprehensively on real-world datasets.

In terms of the algorithm's connections to other techniques, Alternative Review 4 notes that the authors unify LKM, KSUMS, and SC under a general model, which is a significant contribution. However, I would like to see more discussion on the potential applications of this unified model.

On a related note, it would be nice to include some information on the dimensionality and number of points in each dataset listed in 4.2.1 in the main paper, if possible. As the main review notes, the authors use 16 datasets, which seems like a fairly comprehensive set of experiments, but I suspect most readers will not have an idea of what the characteristics of these datasets are.

Additionally, the authors could consider using techniques like those mentioned in the main review, such as tree-based methods or hashing, to quickly construct k-NN graphs. The authors also compare Lloyd's algorithm to LKM, but there are several variants of Lloyd's algorithm that produce exactly the same result in accelerated time, such as Elkan's and Hamerly's algorithms.

Finally, I think the paper could benefit from some careful proofreading. There are numerous grammatical issues, especially having to do with tenses (present/past/etc.). The suggestion in Eq. (10) to convert a (non-metric) similarity function to a dissimilarity function still produces a non-metric: it is not guaranteed to satisfy the triangle inequality.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors mention that LKM may not work well in cases where k-NN graphs are not available, and that a graph construction algorithm is necessary. However, they do not discuss potential negative societal impacts of the proposed algorithm, such as bias in clustering results or potential misuse in applications like surveillance or marketing.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
